q3: The closing line was the persistent sticking point — the model kept landing on something that felt like a promise of clarity or a status update rather than actual forward motion. It understood the structural arc reasonably well but kept overshooting or undershooting the ending, which required a lot of iterative nudging.
q5: I looked at it, and honestly it's a pretty good articulation of what I care about — 'reader-centric framing' and 'action-oriented endings' are real priorities for me. But the rubric describes the destination without helping the model feel when it's arrived. The model could reference all six criteria and still produce a closing line that technically checks out but doesn't land right.
q6: The rubric made me realize the model is consistently better at structure than at tone calibration — it gets the order right and the ownership right, but the final sentence keeps slipping into something that feels like reassurance rather than motion. Without the rubric labeling these as separate dimensions, I might have just said 'the ending is off' without noticing it's specifically a tone calibration failure, not a structural one.

q3: The middle section kept defaulting to logistics lists instead of landing the emotional weight of the disruption. Even after multiple rounds of feedback, it would describe what changes rather than what it means for the reader — that gap between 'here's what shifts' and 'here's why that actually matters to you' took a lot of iterations to close.
q5: I did look at it, and honestly the rubric captured most of what I care about — reader-centric framing, structural arc, action-oriented endings. It was closer to my actual preferences than I expected. Whether it made the model perform better is less clear — I still had to give the same kinds of corrections I would have without it, so the rubric may have helped frame the criteria but didn't seem to accelerate how fast the model got there.
q6: The rubric made me notice that the model consistently nailed the opening and closing beats faster than the middle — once I flagged the acknowledgment issue it adjusted quickly, and the closing was clean early. The middle 'stakes' section was the persistent weak spot across every draft, which I might have just chalked up to 'needs more work' without the rubric making it clear that reader-centric framing was supposed to be the top priority and was still being missed there.

q3: The model kept struggling with the structural arc — specifically, it would sometimes drop the acknowledgment beat or place it after the impact beat instead of before it. It understood the individual pieces but had trouble getting the order right without me pointing it out each time.
q5: I glanced at it, and honestly it captured a lot of what I care about — reader-centric framing and the structural arc are both in there. But the rubric describes the 'what' without really conveying the 'feel' of why the order matters, so the model still needed my corrections to internalize it in practice.
q6: Seeing the rubric made me realize the model was consistently stronger on the ending (action-oriented close) than the opening (acknowledgment beat) — it seemed to default to leading with impact or news rather than grounding first, which tracks with every round of feedback I gave.

q3: The mid-section framing kept defaulting to sender-centric reasoning before I pushed back — 'worth capturing it properly' is about process hygiene, not about the reader's reality. It also kept hedging on ownership in action items, which required explicit correction each time.
q5: I looked at it, and honestly it's a pretty good translation of what I care about — the reader-centric framing criterion and the arc structure are real. But 'Clear Ownership' as written still left wiggle room for the model to over-specify org structure details, which is the exact thing I hate. The rubric captured the 'name one owner' part but missed the 'don't assume their org chart' nuance.
q6: Seeing the rubric made me notice the model was consistently stronger on structure and closing than on the middle stakes beat — it would nail the arc order but then write the 'why it matters' sentence from my perspective instead of the reader's, almost every time. Without the rubric I probably would have just called it a 'tone thing' rather than recognizing it as a framing pattern.

q3: The structural order kept slipping — the stakes sentence kept landing in the wrong place relative to the task list, and it took multiple rounds to get that fixed. The model understood the individual pieces but struggled to hold the sequence together without me explicitly calling it out each time.
q5: I looked at it, and honestly it captured most of what I care about pretty well — reader-centric framing at the top, structural arc, action-oriented endings. What it didn't fully capture is how rigid my sequencing instinct is. 'Stakes before action' is in there, but the rubric frames it loosely enough that the model kept interpreting it as 'stakes somewhere near the action' rather than 'stakes immediately above the next steps, pulling forward.' Close but not quite.
q6: The rubric made me notice that the model is actually pretty good at the ownership piece — single owner, deadline, no floating group asks — and I think that's where the rubric's specificity helped most. It also made me realize the model's default failure mode is structural drift: it gets each element right in isolation but loses the thread of how they connect when revising.
